essentials.heading: Reliability, scalability & maintainability on AWS
essentials.body: Martin Kleppmann frames every data system around three goals. **Reliability** = keeps working correctly despite faults — on AWS this is **Multi-AZ** deployments, health checks, automated retries, and removing single points of failure. **Scalability** = handles growth in load — **horizontal scale-out** (Auto Scaling groups, stateless compute behind an ALB, sharded data stores) beats vertical scale-up because a single box has a ceiling. **Maintainability** = easy to operate and evolve — managed services, **Infrastructure as Code** (CloudFormation/Pulumi), and clear service boundaries. A data-intensive app is built from composable parts: a layer that stores data (databases, object stores), one that speeds reads (caches), one that searches (indexes), and one that processes streams or batches. The architect's job is choosing the right tool per layer, not forcing one database to do everything.

diagram.caption: A layered data-intensive architecture: edge → stateless compute → stateful data stores → derived analytics. Each layer scales independently.

essentials.heading: Replication topologies & how AWS data stores map to them
essentials.body: Replication keeps copies of data on multiple nodes for fault tolerance, lower read latency, and higher read throughput. **Single-leader (leader-follower)**: all writes go to one leader, which streams changes to read-only followers — this is **RDS Multi-AZ + read replicas** and **Aurora** (one writer, up to 15 readers over shared storage). Reads scale on replicas, but replicas lag, so a read right after a write may be stale (replication lag). **Multi-leader**: several nodes accept writes and converge — **DynamoDB global tables** replicate writes across regions with last-writer-wins conflict resolution, giving local low-latency writes worldwide. **Leaderless/quorum**: any node accepts reads/writes and consistency comes from requiring overlapping quorums (W + R > N). Synchronous replication (Aurora storage, Multi-AZ standby) protects against data loss on failover; asynchronous replication (read replicas, CRR) is faster but can lose the last writes on failure.

diagram.caption: Single-leader (RDS/Aurora), multi-leader (DynamoDB global tables) and leaderless quorum topologies.

essentials.heading: Partitioning (sharding) & avoiding hot partitions
essentials.body: When data is too big or too hot for one node, you **partition** it: each row lives on the partition chosen by its key. DynamoDB hashes the **partition key** to place items and spreads provisioned (or on-demand) throughput across partitions. The golden rule from DDIA: choose a **high-cardinality, uniformly accessed** partition key so load spreads evenly. A low-cardinality key (e.g. `status = 'active'`) or a monotonically increasing key (timestamps) creates a **hot partition** — one partition gets most traffic and throttles even though the table has spare capacity. Fixes: pick a key with many distinct, evenly-used values (user_id, order_id), add a random/calculated **suffix** to spread writes, or use a composite key. Use a **sort key** to model one-to-many relationships within a partition, and **Global Secondary Indexes** for alternate access patterns. This same hash-partitioning idea underlies Kinesis shards and Redshift distribution keys.

diagram.caption: A high-cardinality key spreads load evenly; a low-cardinality key concentrates traffic on one hot partition.

essentials.heading: Consistency models: strong vs eventual
essentials.body: Distributed reads force a trade-off between freshness and latency/availability. **Eventual consistency**: a read may return a stale value for a short window after a write, but the system stays fast and highly available — the default for **DynamoDB reads** and for reads served by **RDS/Aurora read replicas**. **Strong consistency**: a read always reflects the latest committed write. DynamoDB offers it as an opt-in (`ConsistentRead=true`, costing more RCUs and only within a region). **Amazon S3** now provides **strong read-after-write consistency** for all GET/PUT/LIST automatically. Know the exam pattern: if a scenario needs the absolute latest data (e.g. a balance check immediately after a debit) you need a strongly-consistent read or read from the primary, not a replica; if slight staleness is acceptable, eventual reads scale cheaper and faster. This mirrors the CAP intuition — under a network partition you cannot have both perfect consistency and full availability.

essentials.heading: Batch vs stream processing pipelines
essentials.body: Derived data (analytics, search indexes, ML features) is produced two ways. **Batch processing** runs over a bounded, large dataset on a schedule — high throughput, high latency. On AWS: read raw data from **S3**, transform with **AWS Glue** (serverless Spark) or **EMR**, write results back to S3 or **Redshift**, query with **Athena**. **Stream processing** handles an unbounded sequence of events as they arrive — low latency, continuous. On AWS: producers write to **Kinesis Data Streams** or **Amazon MSK (Kafka)**, consumed by **Lambda**, Kinesis Data Analytics (Flink), or KCL apps, landing in DynamoDB/S3 for real-time dashboards and alerting. Kinesis partitions events across **shards** by partition key (same hot-key concern as above). A common modern design is the unifying idea from DDIA: treat the **log/stream as the source of truth** and derive both the real-time and the batch views from it.

diagram.caption: Batch reprocesses bounded datasets on a schedule; streaming handles unbounded events continuously on arrival.

essentials.heading: Decoupling for scalability & fault isolation
essentials.body: Tightly coupled components fail and scale together; decoupling lets each part absorb load and fail independently — a core resilience technique. Put a **queue (SQS)** between a producer and a slow consumer so spikes are buffered and consumers scale on queue depth; use a **fan-out topic (SNS)** or **EventBridge** to deliver one event to many subscribers. This turns synchronous request chains into asynchronous, retryable message flows with **dead-letter queues** for poison messages. Combined with **idempotent** consumers, this gives at-least-once processing without data corruption. The architectural payoff: back-pressure instead of cascading failure, independent scaling per component, and the freedom to evolve services behind stable message contracts.

labs.title: Single-leader replication: Aurora writer + read replica
labs.intro: Create an **Aurora MySQL** cluster (one writer) and add a **reader instance** in another AZ. This is single-leader replication: writes hit the writer, reads scale on the reader via the cluster's reader endpoint.
labs.cli:
aws rds create-db-cluster --db-cluster-identifier saa-arch-cluster --engine aurora-mysql --master-username admin --manage-master-user-password --vpc-security-group-ids sg-xxxx --db-subnet-group-name my-subnet-group

# Writer (leader)
aws rds create-db-instance --db-instance-identifier saa-arch-writer --db-cluster-identifier saa-arch-cluster --engine aurora-mysql --db-instance-class db.t3.medium

# Reader (follower) in a different AZ
aws rds create-db-instance --db-instance-identifier saa-arch-reader --db-cluster-identifier saa-arch-cluster --engine aurora-mysql --db-instance-class db.t3.medium

# App reads via the reader endpoint, writes via the cluster endpoint
aws rds describe-db-clusters --db-cluster-identifier saa-arch-cluster --query 'DBClusters[0].[Endpoint,ReaderEndpoint]'
labs.pulumi:
import pulumi_aws as aws

cluster = aws.rds.Cluster(
    "saa-arch-cluster",
    cluster_identifier="saa-arch-cluster",
    engine="aurora-mysql",
    master_username="admin",
    manage_master_user_password=True,
    db_subnet_group_name="my-subnet-group",
    skip_final_snapshot=True,
)

writer = aws.rds.ClusterInstance(
    "saa-arch-writer",
    cluster_identifier=cluster.id,
    engine="aurora-mysql",
    instance_class="db.t3.medium",
)

reader = aws.rds.ClusterInstance(
    "saa-arch-reader",
    cluster_identifier=cluster.id,
    engine="aurora-mysql",
    instance_class="db.t3.medium",
)

import pulumi
pulumi.export("writer_endpoint", cluster.endpoint)
pulumi.export("reader_endpoint", cluster.reader_endpoint)
labs.note: **Cleanup**: delete instances first, then the cluster (`aws rds delete-db-instance ...` ×2, then `aws rds delete-db-cluster --db-cluster-identifier saa-arch-cluster --skip-final-snapshot`), or `pulumi destroy`. Reader replicas lag the writer slightly — a read immediately after a write on the reader endpoint may be stale.

labs.title: Partitioning: DynamoDB on-demand table with a high-cardinality key
labs.intro: Create an **on-demand DynamoDB table** keyed on a high-cardinality `user_id` (partition key) + `created_at` (sort key). On-demand spreads throughput automatically; the good key choice keeps load even and avoids hot partitions.
labs.cli:
aws dynamodb create-table \
  --table-name saa-arch-events \
  --attribute-definitions AttributeName=user_id,AttributeType=S AttributeName=created_at,AttributeType=S \
  --key-schema AttributeName=user_id,KeyType=HASH AttributeName=created_at,KeyType=RANGE \
  --billing-mode PAY_PER_REQUEST

# Strongly-consistent read (latest value) vs default eventual read
aws dynamodb get-item --table-name saa-arch-events --key '{"user_id":{"S":"u-123"},"created_at":{"S":"2026-06-12T10:00:00Z"}}' --consistent-read
labs.pulumi:
import pulumi_aws as aws

table = aws.dynamodb.Table(
    "saa-arch-events",
    name="saa-arch-events",
    billing_mode="PAY_PER_REQUEST",
    hash_key="user_id",
    range_key="created_at",
    attributes=[
        {"name": "user_id", "type": "S"},
        {"name": "created_at", "type": "S"},
    ],
)

import pulumi
pulumi.export("table_name", table.name)
labs.note: **Cleanup**: `aws dynamodb delete-table --table-name saa-arch-events` or `pulumi destroy`. Note: `user_id` is high-cardinality so writes spread evenly; keying on something like `status` would funnel traffic to a hot partition and throttle despite spare capacity.

questions.stem: A read-heavy reporting feature runs analytical queries against an Amazon RDS PostgreSQL primary, slowing down the transactional workload. The reports can tolerate data that is a few seconds stale. What is the most appropriate way to scale reads?
options: Add RDS read replicas and direct the reporting queries to them
Enable Multi-AZ and run the reports against the standby
Increase the primary instance size repeatedly (scale up)
Switch the reports to strongly-consistent reads on the primary
questions.explanation: Correct: read replicas use single-leader (asynchronous) replication to offload read traffic from the primary, and a few seconds of replication lag is acceptable here. The Multi-AZ standby is for failover only and cannot serve reads. Scaling up has a hard ceiling and doesn't isolate the analytical load. Forcing reads onto the primary keeps the contention you are trying to remove.

questions.stem: A DynamoDB table uses a partition key of `country_code`. Most traffic comes from two countries, and the application is being throttled even though overall provisioned/on-demand capacity looks sufficient. What is the root cause and best fix?
options: A hot partition from low key cardinality; choose a higher-cardinality key such as user_id (optionally with a suffix)
Insufficient read capacity units; double the provisioned RCUs
Missing Global Secondary Index; add a GSI on country_code
Eventual consistency; switch all reads to strongly-consistent
questions.explanation: Correct: a low-cardinality partition key concentrates traffic on a few partitions (a hot partition), so requests throttle while other partitions sit idle. The fix is a high-cardinality, evenly-accessed key (e.g. user_id), optionally with a write-sharding suffix. Adding capacity doesn't help because the bottleneck is one partition, not total capacity. A GSI on the same skewed attribute keeps the skew. Read consistency is unrelated to partition skew.

questions.stem: A banking app debits an account in DynamoDB and immediately reads the balance to display it. The displayed balance must always reflect the just-completed write. Which read setting is required?
options: A strongly-consistent read (ConsistentRead=true)
An eventually-consistent read for lower latency
A read against a DynamoDB global table replica in another region
A read served from DAX without write-through
questions.explanation: Correct: a strongly-consistent read returns the most recent committed write, which is mandatory when stale data is unacceptable. Eventually-consistent reads may briefly return the pre-debit value. A cross-region global table replica is updated asynchronously and can be stale. A cache without write-through can also serve a stale balance.

questions.stem: A global application needs low-latency writes for users in North America, Europe, and Asia, with each region able to keep operating if another region is unreachable. Which AWS data design fits best?
options: DynamoDB global tables (multi-leader, multi-region active-active)
A single-region RDS Multi-AZ deployment
An Aurora cluster with cross-region read replicas only
S3 Cross-Region Replication of a JSON state file
questions.explanation: Correct: DynamoDB global tables use multi-leader replication so every region accepts local writes (low latency) and continues operating independently, converging with last-writer-wins. Single-region RDS Multi-AZ gives no local writes elsewhere and is a regional failure domain. Cross-region read replicas accept writes only in the primary region. Replicating a JSON file in S3 is not a concurrent multi-writer database.

questions.stem: A team must compute daily aggregates over terabytes of clickstream logs already stored in S3. The job runs once per night and latency is not critical, but it must be cost-effective and require minimal cluster management. Which approach fits the batch-processing need best?
options: Transform with AWS Glue (serverless Spark) and query results with Athena
Stream every historical log line through Kinesis Data Streams to Lambda
Load all logs into DynamoDB and scan them nightly
Run the aggregation on a single large EC2 instance with a cron job
questions.explanation: Correct: a scheduled aggregation over a bounded, large dataset is classic batch processing; Glue provides serverless Spark (no cluster to manage) over S3, and Athena queries the output cheaply. Kinesis is for unbounded real-time streams, not reprocessing historical batches. DynamoDB scans of TBs are expensive and slow. A single EC2 instance doesn't scale to terabytes and reintroduces server management.

questions.stem: An order service calls a slow downstream fulfillment service synchronously. Traffic spikes cause the fulfillment service to be overwhelmed and orders to be lost. Which change improves resilience and lets each component scale independently?
options: Place an SQS queue between the services and have fulfillment consume at its own pace, with a dead-letter queue
Increase the synchronous HTTP timeout on the order service
Move both services onto one larger EC2 instance
Add retries with no backoff from the order service
questions.explanation: Correct: an SQS queue decouples the producer from the slow consumer, buffering spikes (back-pressure) and letting fulfillment scale on queue depth; a dead-letter queue captures poison messages. Raising timeouts still couples them and ties up resources. Co-locating both services couples their scaling and failure. Retries without backoff amplify the overload (retry storm).

questions.stem: An architecture review flags that a web tier, app tier, and database all run in a single Availability Zone. Which set of changes best improves reliability against an AZ failure? (Choose two)
options: Run the app tier in an Auto Scaling group spanning multiple AZs behind an ALB
Enable RDS Multi-AZ so a standby in another AZ can take over on failure
Vertically scale the single database instance to the largest size
Store application state in the local instance store for speed
questions.explanation: Correct: spreading the stateless app tier across AZs in an Auto Scaling group behind an ALB removes the compute single point of failure, and RDS Multi-AZ provides a standby in another AZ for automatic failover. Vertical scaling keeps everything in one AZ — still a single failure domain. Instance store is ephemeral and lost on stop/failure, making state less reliable, not more.

questions.stem: A microservice needs sub-millisecond reads of frequently-requested, rarely-changing reference data that currently comes from a relational database under heavy read load. Which addition best improves read performance and scalability?
options: Add an in-memory cache (ElastiCache) in front of the database for hot reads
Add more columns and indexes to the existing table
Switch the database to strongly-consistent reads everywhere
Move the database to a larger instance class only
questions.explanation: Correct: an in-memory cache such as ElastiCache serves hot, slow-changing data at sub-millisecond latency and absorbs read load, the dedicated 'speed up reads' layer in a data-intensive design. Extra indexes help specific queries but don't provide a separate scalable read layer and can slow writes. Strong consistency adds cost/latency without addressing throughput. Scaling up the DB has a ceiling and doesn't isolate read traffic.
